CFDocument.title: CTAE - Georgia Standards of Excellence: Career Cluster: Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics

CFItems.fullStatement: Foundation of Electronics
Demonstrate employability skills required by business and industry.
Communicate effectively through writing, speaking, listening, reading, and interpersonal abilities.
Demonstrate creativity by asking challenging questions and applying innovative procedures and methods.
Exhibit critical thinking and problem solving skills to locate, analyze and apply information in career planning and employment situations.
Model work readiness traits required for success in the workplace including integrity, honesty, accountability, punctuality, time management, and respect for diversity.
Apply the appropriate skill sets to be productive in a changing, technological, diverse workplace to be able to work independently and apply team work skills.
Present a professional image through appearance, behavior and language.

Develop an understanding of engineering and electronics and describe the principal fields of engineering and electronic specializations (ex. aeronautical, automotive, chemical, civil, industrial, and mechanical, computer software, electrical, and biomedical) and identify associated career tracks.
Explain a contemporary definition of engineering and electronics.
Identify education requirements for engineering and electronics occupations and locations where programs of study are available.
Match engineering and electronics job titles with qualifications and responsibilities.
Participate in activities related to career interests.
Explain how each engineering and electronic discipline will relate to a green environment and sustainability.
Describe and follow safety, health and environmental standards related to Science, Technology, Engineering, and Math (STEM) workplaces.
Implement workplace and product safety standards such as OSHA, EPA, ISO, GMP, and UL. (STEM-ST3).
Accurately interpret safety signs, symbols, and labels (Hazardous Communications).
Demonstrate and incorporate safe laboratory procedures in lab, shop, and field environments.
Explain how the incorporation or lack of safety practices impact the economy and costs of safety in business and industry.
Identify, select, and use appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE), follow work area organization procedures and follow Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) when preforming work.
Identify criteria of usage, care, and maintenance for tools and machines.
Identify, select and use appropriate tools and machines for specific tasks.
Demonstrate safe use of tools and machines.
Use precision tools and instruments to measure and convert units.
Utilize appropriate computer hardware and software to compose, analyze and synthesize data to document the design process.
Apply proper maintenance techniques for tools, machines, and hardware.
Introduce the history and development of electron theory.
Discuss the history of electron theory.
Identify the atom: protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Identify material conductivity/insulators.
Identify electronic theories applicable to electronic processes.
Define Ohm’s law and formula component parts.
Define Kirchoff’s law and component parts.
Define Watt’s law and component parts.
Design and analyze a simple circuit to determine the values of the various electronic component parts.
Demonstrate the use of metric prefixes and value conversions.
Introduce electronic components that comprise an electronic system.
Identify Resistor Color Code and component polarity.
Identify and describe various resistors, capacitors, transistors, coils, semiconductors, etc.
Discuss circuit design and construction.
Develop and evaluate a prototype device.
Introduce the techniques and processes in electronics systems.
Explain and demonstrate basic soldering techniques.
Explain procedures for connecting circuit components.
Conduct laboratory experiments utilizing appropriate soldering techniques.
Evaluate prototype produced.
Understand the various measuring apparatuses appropriate to electronics systems.
Identify and demonstrate proper use of a multi-meter.
Identify and demonstrate proper use of an oscilloscope.
Discuss virtual computer simulation testing and how it is used in electronics.
Construct a continuity prototype device.
Use appropriate technology to collect, record, manipulate, analyze, and report data.
Demonstrate the ability to recognize cause and effect when faced with projects or issues.
Recognize measurable attributes in units, objects, systems, and processes in assigned activities.
Organize data and the consequences of the problems or issues, and research the material placing it in manageable formats.
Attempt to predict the outcomes based on data collected in a project or experiment.
Defend one's position based on quality collection of facts and data supporting plans, processes, and/or projects.
Draw a conclusion when confronted with data or observations that focus on the observed plans, processes, or projects at hand.
Analyze change as a result of data differences and changing environmental values.
Use qualitative and quantitative skills to conduct a simple scientific inquiry and economic analysis; use the data to draw a conclusion based on the analysis.
Recognize the value of the reiterative process to improve data and to improve the design process.
Design a solution to an engineering and electronics problem applying math and science principles.
Apply science and mathematics concepts and principles to resolve plans, projects, processes, issues, or problems through methods of inquiry.
Use the protocols in science and mathematics to integrate solutions related to technical, electronic, or engineering activities using the content and concepts related to the situation or problems.
Explain the role of modeling and/or simulation in electricity and electronics.
Communicate and collaborate with others on inquiry or resolution of issues/problems in the global community.
Defend one's solution based on quality collection of facts and data supporting plans, processes, and/or projects and communicate the solution both orally and written.
Construct an electronic device as a culminating experience.
Construct Series, Parallel and Series/Parallel circuits.
Simulate test circuits utilizing electronic software.
Design, construct, and test an electronic device from component parts.

Explore how related career and technology student organizations are integral parts of career and technology education courses. Students will develop leadership, interpersonal, and problem-solving skills through participation in co-curricular activities associated with the Technology Student Association.
Explain the goals, mission and objectives of CTSO’s.

Explore the impact and opportunities a student organization (TSA) can develop to bring business and education together in a positive working relationship through innovative leadership and career development programs.

Explore the local, state, and national opportunities available to students through participation in related student organization (TSA) including but not limited to conferences, competitions, community service, philanthropy, and other (TSA) activities.
Explain how participation in career and technology education student organizations can promote lifelong responsibility for community service and professional development.
Demonstrate teamwork, leadership, interpersonal relations, and project management.
Through teamwork, apply the skills and abilities in requirements analysis and configuration control while working with plans, processes, and projects as assigned.
Through teamwork, use the skills required in project management to track and assess the progress of a plan, process, or project as assigned.
Through teamwork, apply the skills in quality assurance as well as those in process management and development for appropriate applications of systems integration techniques to an assigned project.

Effectively use project management techniques (e.g., teamwork, appropriate time management practices, effective organizational skills, conduct analysis of cost, resources, and production capacity, and quality practices with continuous improvement).
Understand and demonstrate proper work ethics when working with plans, processes, and projects as assigned.
Advanced AC and DC Circuits
CFItems.notes:
**Course Description:** As the second course in the Electronics Pathway, this course is designed for students interested in careers related to the design, production, analysis, repair, and operation of devices that use electronics. The course is designed around major individual and class projects that promote critical thinking, real world problem solving, and abstract reasoning that encourage the student to become an investigative lifelong learner. Students will create artifacts that demonstrate application of competencies in technical, academic, cognitive, and personal skills through daily work, team work, and homework, formative and informative assessments. The prerequisite for this course is Foundations of Electronics.

**Document Date:** October 11, 2013

Apply analog circuits.

Evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of analog signaling.
Predict the future of analog electronics.
Research and present operational characteristics and applications of amplifiers.
Define and discuss power supplies.
Technically sketch or draw and construct a power supply circuit
Define and discuss the different types of transistors (Bipolar junction transistor or BJT and metal oxide semiconductor or MOS).

Demonstrate negative feedback differential amplifiers.
Conduct analysis and troubleshooting.

Technically sketch or draw and construct full-wave devices.

Create a digital project that displays mastery of the standards involved with electronics.

Make recommendations for improvements on each project based on experiences gained from the process.

Identify numbering systems used in digital electronics and calculate conversion between systems.
Apply Boolean concepts to simplification processes using DeMorgan laws for transformation or Karmaugh Maps (K Maps).
Demonstrate knowledge of logic gates (IF, Then, Else).
Classify and compare names, symbols, truth tables and Boolean Expression for the following logic gates: AND, OR, NOT, NOR, NAND (Negated AND or NOT AND), exclusive OR, and exclusive NOR gates.

Classify and compare names, symbols, truth tables and Boolean Expression for the logic gates that include practical Transistor-Transistor Logic (TTL) and Complementary Metal Oxide Semiconductor (CMOS).
Classify and compare names, symbols, truth tables and Boolean Expression for the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) logic symbols.
Compare and contrast the use of several commonly used digital codes, including the differences between conversion of decimal numbers and letters to code.
Define and create a compare and contrast chart for 8421 Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD), Excess-3, and American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII) codes.
Identify and discuss applications for encoders/decoders.
Construct a technical sketch or draw a display and/or multiplexers.
Determine the best code for specific industry problems.
Use truth tables and interpret waveforms to determine flip-flop modes of operation and outputs.
Technically sketch or draw RS, D, and JK flip-flop circuits.
Technically sketch or draw Integrated Circuits (IC) Latches and Schmitt Triggers.
Create a project that demonstrates knowledge of operation and outputs.
Analyze the output for a variety of counters based on a series of inputs.
Estimate output and then measure by ripple, synchronous, down, and self-stopping counters. Compile information into a table and present that suggests the most accurate method.
Graph and analyze the data for frequency dividers.
Interpret shift registers through critiquing the results.
Create counter circuits to solve real world scenarios.
Analyze block-style logic diagrams.
Technically sketch or draw parallel adder circuits.
Technically sketch or draw parallel subtractor circuits.
Create a project that states the advantages and disadvantages of parallel added and subtracted circuits and includes binary multiplication.
Investigate common memory and storage devices used in a microcomputer system.
Assess the value for common memory, microcomputer function block diagram and storage devices used in a microcomputer system.

Create a project that demonstrates the traits of Random Access Memory (RAM), Static RAM, Read-Only Memory (ROM), Programmable Read-Only Memory (PROMs), Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), and registers, control and data path.
Discuss non-volatile read/write in a project.
Compare various types of portable or temporary memory devices.
Create a digital project that displays mastery of the standards involved with electronics.
Present the constructed projects from the standards in a digital portfolio through pictures, drawings, data and analysis.
Make recommendations for improvements on each project based on experiences gained from the process.

Engineering Concepts
CFItems.notes:
**Course Description:**
Engineering Concepts is the second course in the Engineering and Technology Pathway. Students will learn to design technical solutions to engineering problems using a whole systems approach to engineering design. Students will demonstrate the application of mathematical tools, teamwork, and communications skills in solving various design challenges, while maintaining a safe work environment. The prerequisite for this course is Foundations of Engineering and Technology.

**Document Date:**
October 11, 2013

Assess and evaluate prototype testing data to recommend design improvements, optimization, or re-design of prototype.
Understand engineering impacts of social, economic, design and environmental issues.
Apply knowledge of external issues such as time constraints, budget, supply chain and available technology that strain the engineering design process to optimize a solution to a STEM problem.
Analyze and connect the impacts of events in the global marketplace to understand the importance of national standards, supply chains, and timelines.
Analyze the sustainability and life cycle of an engineered product and their applications on a worldwide scale.
Connect cultural diversity to possible impacts on creating solutions to engineering design problems.
Explain the impact of business and marketing on engineering design.
Gather and synthesize information using social media and the internet.
Research the global nature of engineering design in multinational corporations.
Demonstrate an understanding of the design timeline, time to market, and the impact of a rapidly changing consumer market.
Generate and analyze market research in terms of consumer requirements, competitive landscape, and market opportunity.
Develop iterative accounting analysis for engineering designs such as cost analysis, return on investment, Bill of Materials, and labor and production costs using appropriate spreadsheet software.
Apply supply and demand economics to determine market pricing.
Create and present marketing plans to peers, decision makers, and potential investors.

Explore how related career and technology student organizations are integral parts of career and technology education courses. Students will develop leadership, interpersonal, and problem-solving skills through participation in cocurricular activities associated with the Technology Student Association (TSA).
Explain the goals, mission and objectives of Career Technical Student Organizations (CTSOs).

Explore the impact and opportunities a student organization (TSA) can develop to bring business and education together in a positive working relationship through innovative leadership and career development programs.

Explore the local, state, and national opportunities available to students through participation in related student organization (TSA) including but not limited to conferences, competitions, community service, philanthropy, and other (TSA) activities.
Explain how participation in career and technology education student organizations can promote lifelong responsibility for community service and professional development.
Demonstrate teamwork, leadership, interpersonal relations, and project management.
Through teamwork, apply the skills and abilities in requirements analysis and configuration control while working with plans, processes, and projects as assigned.
Through teamwork, use the skills required in project management to track and assess the progress of a plan, process, or project as assigned.
Through teamwork, apply the skills in quality assurance as well as those in process management and development for appropriate applications of systems integration techniques to an assigned project.

Effectively use project management techniques (e.g., teamwork, appropriate time management practices, effective organizational skills, conduct analysis of cost, resources, and production capacity, and quality practices with continuous improvement).
Understand and demonstrate proper work ethics when working with plans, processes, and projects as assigned.
Survey of Engineering Graphics
CFItems.notes:
**Course Description:**
Survey of Engineering Graphics is the second course in the Engineering Drafting and Design Career Pathway. The course is designed to build student skills and knowledge in the field of engineering graphics/technical drafting. The course focus includes employability skills, career opportunities, applied math, working drawings that include sectional, auxiliary, detail and pictorial views, and pattern developments. In addition, elements in applied mathematics are integrated throughout the course. The prerequisite for this course is Introduction to Drafting & Design.

**Document Date:**
October 11, 2013

Present a professional image through appearance, behavior and language.

Demonstrate and follow safety, health, and environmental standards related to the STEM workplace and apply specific engineering tools, machines, materials and processes in a safe and orderly manner to formulate, analyze, and verify engineering practices and solutions.

Participate in activities related to career interests.
Analyze an ethical situation related to engineering graphics and engineering.
Maintain a journal that relates standards in the course to the project work.
Analyze applied math required by business and industry for engineering graphics.

Analyze and apply correct tolerance in regards to (American National Standard for Information Systems) ANSI and National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and other international bodies that control standards with the correct use of geometric constraints and symbols.
Estimate and measure using metric and imperial scale. Compare the estimate with the actual results and analyze.
Calculate the ratio and scale for specific problems.
Construct conversions.
Identify and describe the correct units on existing drawings.
Measure using an engineering Scale and basic rulers.
Present how to determine the appropriate tool use for measurements.
Demonstrate purpose and correct application of sectional views.
Identify and explain sectional views: full, half, offset, revolved, removed and brokenout sections.
Determine the six sectional views from provided drawings.
Create technical freehand sketch of a sectional view.
Prepare drawings that require sectional views.
Recommend materials for sectional views including hatching patterns and appropriate symbols based on strength and product requirements.
Read and reproduce sectional view blueprint.
Create a sectional view from an existing multi-view drawing.
Demonstrate purpose and correct application of Auxiliary views.
Identify and explain primary and secondary auxiliary views.
Recommend applications or purpose of auxiliary views for specific drawings or objects.
Create technical freehand sketch of an auxiliary view.
Prepare drawings that require auxiliary views.
Read, revise and produce auxiliary blueprint.
Demonstrate purpose and correct application of pictorial views.
Identify and describe isometric and isometric exploded pictorial drawings.
Determine applications or purpose of pictorial drawings.
Create technical freehand sketch of pictorial drawings.
Prepare drawings that require pictorial view.
Read, revise and produce pictorial blueprint or an existing object or drawing.
Cite evidence of developments in engineering graphics and engineering.
Identify and describe welding, sheet metal and geometric shapes as related to the general principals of pattern development.
Analyze applications or purpose of developments and patterns.
Apply concepts for various geometric shapes to patterns.
Present appropriate views of an object.
Create a table that states the advantages and disadvantages of sectional, auxiliary, pictorial views and save electronically and in a portfolio.
Insert at least one drawing from the course that demonstrates sectional, auxiliary, and pictorial views into the portfolio and save electronically.
Create an original object and generate sectional, auxiliary and pictorial views.
Place in the portfolio and save electronically.
Review journal entries and write a short statement about what has been learned about tolerance, appropriate tool use for measurement, sectional view, auxiliary view, pictorial view, and developments.

Apply knowledge of strength of materials in determining the appropriate fastener.
Produce a working drawing artifact that conveys all of the information needed to manufacture and assemble a design.
Demonstrate an understanding of what drawings are required to accurately present an object.
Create a project that demonstrates the impact of tension and compression on an object from a working drawing.
Orally present an understanding of callouts or balloons on working drawings.
Identify and explain important components required on a bill of materials or part list.

Produce a set of working drawings based on an assembled object.

Evaluate and develop assembly drawings.
Demonstrate an understanding of the purpose and application for assembly drawings.
Create an original title block.
Determine when auxiliary or sectional views are required in an assembly drawing.
Write a short paragraph that describes when a subassembly drawing is necessary in an assembly drawing.
Demonstrate how information on the Bill of Materials relates back to the assembly drawing.
Create technical freehand sketch of an assembly drawing.
Construct accurate drawing representations of a 3D assembly model.
Construct a 3D assembly model showing criteria, constraints, design, and quality of a final product by creating a presentation or capstone final project.
Identify and explain the purposes and uses of extracting geometric data from surfaces and wireframes.
Create a chart that shows what drawings are necessary to produce products based on characteristics of the product such as inclined, materials, and fasteners.
Identify the purpose and uses of rendering a model’s image.
Demonstrate an understanding of the application of mass and density of materials when designing an object.
Render an image of a model.
Shade a rendered image of a model.
Animate an image of a model.
Create a summary for an analysis of the object.
Create a presentation of a model that communicates material, finish, mass, and density.

Incorporate all of pathway standards into a capstone project based on the model presentation (including material, finish, mass, and density). Required working and detail drawings like auxiliary and sectional views are to be included in the final project.
